concepts.finding:
We demonstrate that a handful of distilled images can be used to train a model with a ﬁxed initialization
to achieve surprisingly high performance.
concepts.implication: General ML methodology applicable to content recommendation

concepts.finding:
Extensive experiments on four publicly available datasets, MNIST, CIFAR10, PASCAL-VOC, and
CUB-200, show that our approach often outperforms existing methods.
concepts.implication: General ML methodology applicable to content recommendation

concepts.finding:
This advantage makes our method potentially effective for online training
algorithms and useful for the case where malicious users hijack the data feeding pipeline for only one
gradient step (e.g., one network transmission).
concepts.implication: General ML methodology applicable to content recommendation

concepts.finding:
Our method
outperforms few-shot domain adaptation (Motiian et al., 2017) and other baselines in most settings.
concepts.implication: General ML methodology applicable to content recommendation

concepts.finding:
For example, we show that it is possible to compress 60, 000
MNIST training images into just 10 synthetic distilled images (one per class) and
achieve close to original performance with only a few gradient descent steps, given
a ﬁxed network initialization.
concepts.implication: General ML methodology applicable to content recommendation

concepts.finding:
We show that we
can go as low as one synthetic image per category, training the same model to reach surprisingly
good performance on these synthetic images.
concepts.implication: General ML methodology applicable to content recommendation

concepts.finding:
Finally, we study a simple linear model, deriving a lower bound on the
size of distilled data required to achieve the same performance as training on the full dataset.
concepts.implication: General ML methodology applicable to content recommendation
